questions.text: A 68-year-old male with COPD is being evaluated for long-term oxygen therapy. Which ABG parameter is most indicative of chronic hypoxemia and would support the need for home oxygen?
Before performing a radial artery puncture for ABG, which of the following is the correct technique for performing the Modified Allen test?

answers.text: Compress both ulnar and radial arteries, release ulnar, observe palmar flush within 10 seconds
Compress only the radial artery and observe capillary refill in the nailbed
Compress both arteries, release radial, observe palmar flush within 15 seconds
Apply a tourniquet and palpate for radial pulse distal to the site

questions.text: Which of the following is an absolute contraindication to performing a radial artery puncture?

answers.text: Presence of a weak radial pulse

questions.text: A blood gas sample drawn from a radial artery line shows a PaO₂ of 85 mm Hg while the patient is on 4 L/min nasal cannula. However, the sample was drawn from the distal port of a saline lock. What is the most likely cause of these results?

answers.text: The sample was contaminated with heparin
The sample represents venous blood due to peripheral vasoconstriction
The sample was diluted with saline flush solution
The patient was hyperventilating during the draw

questions.text: What is the primary purpose of adding heparin to an ABG syringe?

answers.text: Prevent clot formation in the sample
Reduce hemolysis of red blood cells

questions.text: An ABG sample that has been stored at room temperature for 45 minutes will most likely show which of the following changes compared to a sample analyzed immediately?

answers.text: No significant change in blood gas values

questions.text: When performing a femoral artery puncture, which of the following complications is of greatest concern?
A capillary blood gas sample from a heel stick in an infant shows a pH of 7.32, PaCO₂ 48 mm Hg, and PaO₂ of 48 mm Hg. How should the PaO₂ value be interpreted compared to an arterial sample?

answers.text: It overestimates arterial PaO₂ by about 10 mm Hg
It underestimates arterial PaO₂ by about 5–10 mm Hg
It is unreliable and should not be interpreted

questions.text: A patient has an indwelling radial arterial line. The nurse notices the waveform appears dampened and the flush solution will not return blood when the system is aspirated. Which of the following is the most likely cause?

answers.text: A clot at the catheter tip
The transducer is positioned too low
The stopcock is open to air

questions.text: When using an arterial line for blood pressure monitoring, where should the transducer be positioned for the most accurate reading?

answers.text: At the level of the heart at the mid-axillary line (phlebostatic axis)
At the level of the catheter insertion site
10 cm above the level of the heart
At the level of the mid-chest posteriorly

questions.text: What is the normal range for arterial pH in healthy adults?
A patient presents with a 3-day history of severe vomiting. ABG shows pH 7.52, PaCO₂ 44 mm Hg, HCO₃⁻ 34 mEq/L. Using the ROME method, what is the acid-base disorder?
A patient with COPD has an ABG showing pH 7.33, PaCO₂ 62 mm Hg, HCO₃⁻ 32 mEq/L. Which of the following best describes this acid-base disorder?
According to the ROME method, if the pH and PaCO₂ are both moving in opposite directions (one up, one down), the primary disorder is:
A patient presents with an ABG showing pH 7.48, PaCO₂ 30 mm Hg, HCO₃⁻ 24 mEq/L. What is the expected compensatory response, and is compensation adequate?

answers.text: No compensation needed; this is an uncompensated respiratory alkalosis
The kidneys should increase HCO₃⁻ reabsorption to compensate
The lungs should increase ventilation to compensate
This represents partial metabolic compensation for a respiratory acidosis

questions.text: Carbon dioxide is transported in the blood in three main forms. Which form accounts for the largest proportion (approximately 70%)?
Which of the following factors causes a LEFT shift of the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve?
The Bohr effect describes the phenomenon where:

answers.text: Increased CO₂ and H⁺ concentration decrease hemoglobin's affinity for oxygen, promoting O₂ unloading in tissues
Increased O₂ concentration promotes CO₂ binding to hemoglobin
Decreased pH increases hemoglobin's affinity for oxygen
Fetal hemoglobin has a higher affinity for oxygen than adult hemoglobin

questions.text: The Haldane effect states that:

answers.text: Deoxygenated hemoglobin has an increased capacity to carry CO₂ and H⁺
Oxygenated hemoglobin binds more CO₂ than deoxygenated hemoglobin
Hemoglobin releases O₂ more readily in acidic environments

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a major buffer system in the body?

A patient on 40% venturi mask has a PaO₂ of 65 mm Hg. Calculate the A–a gradient using the simplified alveolar air equation (assume FiO₂ 0.40, PaCO₂ 40 mm Hg, RQ 0.8). Which of the following best characterizes the patient's oxygenation?

answers.text: PAO₂ = (0.40 × 713) − (40/0.8) = 285 − 50 = 235 mm Hg; A–a gradient = 170 mm Hg — significantly elevated
PAO₂ = 150 mm Hg; A–a gradient = 85 mm Hg — mildly elevated
PAO₂ = 300 mm Hg; A–a gradient = 235 mm Hg — severely elevated
PAO₂ = 200 mm Hg; A–a gradient = 135 mm Hg — moderately elevated

questions.text: The Clark (Severinghaus-type) electrode directly measures which of the following parameters?
A co-oximeter reading shows elevated carboxyhemoglobin (HbCO) of 12% in a patient with no known smoke inhalation. Which of the following conditions could artifactually elevate HbCO measurement?

A patient's ABG shows a normal PaO₂ of 92 mm Hg, but the co-oximeter reveals a methemoglobin (MetHb) level of 25%. The patient is cyanotic. Which of the following is the most appropriate interpretation?

answers.text: The patient's oxygen-carrying capacity is significantly reduced despite normal PaO₂
The cyanosis is due to hypercapnia
The MetHb level is clinically insignificant
The high MetHb increases oxygen delivery to tissues

questions.text: In the context of quality control for blood gas analyzers, a Westgard rule violation where one control value exceeds the mean ± 2 SD on two consecutive levels in the same direction is known as:
A Levey-Jennings chart shows the last 7 data points are all above the mean but within ± 2 SD. According to Westgard rules, this pattern indicates:

answers.text: A random error that can be ignored
A systematic error (trend) flagging a 7x rule violation

questions.text: Which regulatory agency requires blood gas laboratories in the United States to participate in proficiency testing programs?

A point-of-care blood gas analyzer (i-STAT) is being used in the ICU. Compared with a central laboratory analyzer, which of the following is the most significant advantage of POC testing for ABG analysis?

answers.text: Reduced turnaround time leading to faster clinical decisions
Higher accuracy than central lab analyzers
Eliminates the need for quality control

questions.text: In a 1-point calibration of a blood gas analyzer, what is being tested?

answers.text: The analyzer's response at zero gas concentration
The analyzer's response at one known gas concentration to adjust the gain
Both zero and span calibration simultaneously
The analyzer's precision across multiple replicates

questions.text: A patient with suspected pulmonary embolism has a normal PaO₂ on room air but a widened A–a gradient. The most likely explanation is:

answers.text: V/Q mismatch causing inefficient gas exchange despite normal PaO₂ at rest
Diffusion impairment from interstitial lung disease

questions.text: A posterior-anterior (PA) chest X-ray is preferred over an anteroposterior (AP) projection primarily because:

answers.text: The PA view magnifies the cardiac silhouette making abnormalities easier to detect
The PA view produces less magnification of the heart and better lung field clarity
The AP view requires higher radiation dose
The PA view is easier to perform on intubated patients

questions.text: A chest X-ray is evaluated as adequately penetrated when which of the following structures is visible?

answers.text: The vertebral bodies behind the cardiac silhouette

questions.text: On a PA chest X-ray, which of the following findings indicates adequate inspiratory effort?

answers.text: The right hemidiaphragm is at the level of the 10th posterior rib
The heart is less than 50% of the thoracic width

questions.text: A chest X-ray shows the clavicles are asymmetric with the right clavicle appearing more horizontal than the left. The cardiomediastinal silhouette appears shifted to the right. This suggests:

answers.text: The patient is rotated to the right
The X-ray beam was angled cephalad

questions.text: A 25-year-old asymptomatic male presents for a routine pre-operative chest X-ray. The image shows clear lung fields bilaterally, sharp costophrenic angles, a normal cardiac silhouette with a cardiothoracic ratio of 42%, and intact bony structures. What is the most accurate interpretation?

answers.text: Normal chest X-ray with no acute cardiopulmonary abnormality

questions.text: A 22-year-old male presents with acute onset left-sided pleuritic chest pain after a motor vehicle collision. BP 90/60, HR 120, trachea deviated to the right. Based on the chest X-ray findings shown, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old male with congestive heart failure presents with progressive dyspnea and orthopnea. Chest X-ray shows blunting of the right costophrenic angle with a meniscus sign and opacification of the right lower hemithorax. What is the most likely finding?

A 70-year-old female with a history of hypertension presents with acute dyspnea, orthopnea, and bilateral crackles. Chest X-ray shows an enlarged cardiac silhouette (CTR > 55%), perihilar bat-wing opacities, Kerley B lines, and cephalization of pulmonary vessels. What is the most likely diagnosis?

answers.text: Pulmonary edema due to left heart failure

questions.text: A 45-year-old male with septic shock presents with acute hypoxemic respiratory failure requiring mechanical ventilation. PaO₂/FiO₂ ratio is 140. Chest X-ray shows bilateral diffuse alveolar and interstitial opacities with air bronchograms. What is the most likely diagnosis?
On a chest X-ray following endotracheal intubation, the tip of the ETT should be positioned:

answers.text: 2–4 cm above the carina (at the level of the aortic knob)
At the level of the clavicles
Below the carina in the right mainstem bronchus

questions.text: Which of the following chest X-ray findings is most characteristic of COPD (chronic bronchitis / emphysema)?

answers.text: Hyperinflation, flattened diaphragms, increased retrosternal air space
Bilateral dense consolidation with air bronchograms

questions.text: A chest X-ray shows a dense, lobar opacity in the right upper lobe with air bronchograms. The patient has fever, productive cough, and leukocytosis. What is the most likely diagnosis?
How can atelectasis be distinguished from consolidation on chest X-ray?

answers.text: Atelectasis produces a dense opacity with air bronchograms; consolidation does not
Atelectasis causes displacement of fissures and tracheal shift toward the opacity; consolidation does not cause volume loss
Consolidation causes fissure displacement toward the opacity; atelectasis causes tracheal shift away
Atelectasis is always bilateral; consolidation is unilateral

questions.text: A trauma patient arrives after a gunshot wound to the chest. Chest X-ray shows a large left-sided pleural opacity with an air-fluid level. The mediastinum is shifted to the right. What is the most likely diagnosis?

answers.text: Left hemothorax with possible air leak (hemopneumothorax)

questions.text: Which of the following advanced imaging modalities is most useful for detecting a pulmonary embolism?

answers.text: MRI of the chest with contrast

questions.text: A ventilation-perfusion (V/Q) scan shows a matched defect (both ventilation and perfusion are absent in the same region). Which condition is most likely?

answers.text: Pneumonia or COPD with compensatory hypoxic vasoconstriction

questions.text: Which of the following statements about PET scanning in pulmonary imaging is true?

answers.text: PET is the gold standard for diagnosing pulmonary embolism
PET combined with CT (PET/CT) is the most accurate noninvasive method for staging lung cancer
PET uses ionizing radiation at levels exceeding conventional CT
PET scans cannot distinguish malignant from benign nodules

questions.text: In lung ultrasound, the presence of A-lines (horizontal repetition artifacts) in a normally aerated lung will be replaced by B-lines (vertical artifacts) in which of the following conditions?
A patient with multiple rib fractures from a motor vehicle collision presents with paradoxical chest wall movement during inspiration. Which of the following is the correct diagnosis?

A 3-year-old child presents with sudden onset of coughing and respiratory distress while eating peanuts. Chest X-ray shows hyperinflation of the right lung with mediastinal shift to the left. What is the most likely cause?

answers.text: Right mainstem bronchus foreign body aspiration with ball-valve effect
